questions.prompt: The lesson says the most expensive mistake a new tech makes on the platform isn't doing the wrong thing — it's something else. What is it?

choices.label: Forgetting to document the response in the PSA.
choices.rationale: Documentation gaps are real but recoverable. Not the most expensive class.

choices: Doing the right thing on the wrong customer.

choices.label: Replying to the analyst before reading the full Evidence section.
choices.rationale: Different class of mistake (covered in the reading-order lesson).

choices.label: Closing an incident before checking the customer's SLA.
choices.rationale: SLA hygiene matters but isn't the lesson's framing of "most expensive."

questions.explanation: Correct action, wrong organisation — a working endpoint taken offline for a customer who was fine.

questions.prompt: Which two paths get you into an organisation's scoped view?
choices: From the global Incidents list (click the org name on an incident), or from the global Organisations list.

choices.label: From the Reports area or from the Settings area.
choices.rationale: Neither is a primary path. Reports aggregate; Settings isn't an org-entry point.

choices.label: By URL only — there's no UI path into an organisation.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Two UI paths.

choices.label: Only by clicking through from an incident.
choices.rationale: Incomplete — the Organisations list is the second path.

questions.explanation: Path one is the daily reflex; path two is for when you need to look something up before any incident exists.

questions.prompt: The lesson recommends a habit it compares to "the way a driver glances at the speedometer." What is it?
choices: Reading the org name on the portal's header strip before every destructive action, every time.

choices.label: Refreshing the page every few minutes to make sure data is current.
choices.rationale: Not the recommended habit.

choices.label: Saying the customer's name out loud before each click.
choices.rationale: The header-strip glance is silent.

choices.label: Re-logging into the portal at the start of each customer.
choices.rationale: Slow and unnecessary.

questions.explanation: The cost of the glance is zero; the cost of the wrong tenant is hours.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson explicitly warn against keeping two organisation tabs open in adjacent browser windows of the same browser?

choices.label: Browsers leak data between tabs of the same domain, so isolation is needed.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the security model. The reason is operator error.

choices: Same domain, same UI — the brain treats two tabs as one, which is how wrong-customer actions happen.

choices.label: Browser memory usage spikes when both tabs are loaded.
choices.rationale: Performance isn't the reason.

choices.label: Huntress audits tab-switching behaviour.
choices.rationale: Not true.

questions.explanation: Same domain, same UI; peripheral vision blurs the URL and the header.

questions.prompt: A senior says over chat: "Isolate the laptop that just popped on Acme, we're seeing canary indicators." You have two Acmes: Acme Manufacturing and Acme Holdings. The senior didn't disambiguate. What do you do?

choices.label: Pick the Acme you talk to more often; that's probably the one.
choices.rationale: "Probably" is not acceptable on an isolation.

choices: Open the most recent Critical incident, identify which Acme tripped, and confirm the host with the senior before isolating.

choices.label: Isolate the laptop in both Acmes — better safe than sorry.
choices.rationale: Worst response. You've potentially taken a working endpoint offline at the wrong customer.

choices.label: Ask the senior to disambiguate over the phone; chat is too ambiguous for this.
choices.rationale: The incident itself names the affected entity. The senior doesn't need to repeat.

questions.explanation: The incident names the Acme; confirming the host with the senior is the two-second pause that prevents the worst class of error.

questions.prompt: You're hovering over Isolate on the right Acme host. Your phone rings — it's another customer panicking. What do you do?

choices.label: Stay on the line, click Isolate as you talk to the other customer.
choices.rationale: Attention split between two customers is the state where wrong-tenant errors happen.

choices: Tell the other customer you'll call back in five, finish the isolation cleanly with the header strip confirmed, then take the call.

choices.label: Abandon the isolation and take the other customer's call.
choices.rationale: The senior is waiting; abandoning without telling them creates a worse gap than a five-minute callback.

choices.label: Pass the phone call to the senior who asked for the isolation.
choices.rationale: Loads the senior with a call that isn't theirs while leaving the isolation half-done.

questions.explanation: Single-task the destructive action. Five minutes' lag on the other customer is the right trade.

questions.prompt: A peer searches for a hostname in the portal and a result appears. They click through to act on it. What's the most likely failure mode the lesson warns about?
choices: Search results may span organisations; two customers can have the same hostname — confirm the org column before clicking through.

choices.label: Search results are sorted by recency only.
choices.rationale: The lesson doesn't comment on sort order; cross-org collisions are the named warning.

choices.label: Searching is slow and breaks under load.
choices.rationale: Performance isn't the warning.

choices.label: Search uses partial matches that pull in too many results.
choices.rationale: Same-hostname collisions across orgs is the named issue.

questions.explanation: Two customers can have the same hostname (SRV01, EXCHANGE, WS-RECEPTION). Confirm the org column.

questions.prompt: You realise you've just isolated a host on the wrong customer. What's the right next move?

choices.label: Quietly un-isolate and hope nobody noticed.
choices.rationale: Don't try to cover it. The customer needs to be told why their endpoint bounced.

choices: Un-isolate, tell a senior in parallel, document the mistake, expect to inform the customer per the comms playbook.

choices.label: Leave the host isolated until a senior tells you what to do.
choices.rationale: Leaving a working host offline to "wait for instruction" extends a recoverable error.

choices.label: Don't escalate at all — it's a navigation mistake, not an incident.
choices.rationale: The lesson distinguishes "navigated to the wrong place" (quiet self-correction) from "took an action on the wrong customer" (escalation).

questions.explanation: Un-isolate, escalate, document, comms. Don't cover.

questions.prompt: Which of these are destructive actions where the header-strip glance matters? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Running the ITDR compromise playbook on an identity
questions.explanation: The lesson names isolating, approving a remediation, and running the compromise playbook as the three destructive examples. Reading and filtering are not destructive.

questions.prompt: A peer says: "I work two organisations in adjacent tabs all the time; I've never made a wrong-tenant mistake. The rule is overkill." Why is that confidence misleading?
choices: The wrong-tenant error happens silently — same domain, same UI — until the day it costs hours.

choices.label: Huntress logs tab switches and audits accounts that violate the rule.
choices.rationale: Not true.

choices.label: Adjacent tabs are slower to render and reduce throughput.
choices.rationale: Performance isn't the reason.

choices.label: The partner billing model charges per tab session.
choices.rationale: Not true.

questions.explanation: The rule prevents a high-cost error that doesn't happen often, not constant low-cost errors.
